lessons.title: Write exact stdout bytes
lessons.body: Send a formatted record through process.stdout.write so the separator and final newline are deliberate. This also establishes that Node decides runtime behavior after TypeScript annotations are stripped; module syntax gets its own orientation lab.

lessons.title: Tokenize Node standard input
lessons.body: Read fd 0 once as UTF-8, split only when trimmed text is nonempty, convert every token, and add the numbers. The empty-input branch prevents a phantom zero token while preserving the stdin harness used later.

lessons.title: Convert text into primitive values
lessons.body: Parse a name, score, and threshold into string and number values before comparing them. An annotation is removed at runtime, so it documents and checks code but cannot turn input text into a number; const binding and UTF-16 string details are explored in nearby labs.

lessons.title: Rebind an accumulator, keep its label
lessons.body: Keep the label in a const binding and update a numeric total through let. const prevents rebinding that variable; it does not freeze an object stored in the binding.

lessons.title: Trim the name, preserve the score
lessons.body: Remove only one terminal LF or CRLF from the pipe-delimited record, trim the name field, and leave every score character intact. JavaScript strings still use UTF-16 code units for length and indexing.

lessons.title: Return a named total as a tuple
lessons.body: Gather the numeric tail of stdin into an array, total it, and place the name and sum in a fixed-position tuple. Tuples remain ordinary Arrays at runtime; iterable consumption and map/filter/reduce are optional follow-on labs.

lessons.title: Consume an Iterable with for-of
lessons.body: Accept Iterable<string> and concatenate values through for...of. An iterable need not support numeric indexing, and an iterator obtained from one may be single-pass rather than reusable.

lessons.title: Filter, square, and reduce safely
lessons.body: Select even values, square the survivors, and fold them with an explicit zero initial value. Supplying that initializer makes an empty filtered array a defined case rather than a reduce exception.

lessons.title: Calculate through a structural object
lessons.body: Parse dimensions into a Rectangle-shaped value and pass it to an area function. TypeScript checks compatible members rather than nominal identity; class runtime state and shallow readonly views remain optional labs.

lessons.title: Update state behind a class method
lessons.body: Store a counter value in a class and change it only through increment. TypeScript private is a checker restriction that is erased; JavaScript #private fields, by contrast, enforce a runtime boundary.

lessons.title: Read a shallow readonly configuration
lessons.body: Accept a readonly name and readonly numeric sequence, then total the sequence without mutation. readonly is type-only and shallow: it neither freezes the runtime object nor recursively protects nested mutable values.

lessons.title: Keep an inclusive loop boundary
lessons.body: Sum odd integers from one through n with an explicit branch and loop bound. Strict equality avoids coercion, while <= rather than < decides whether an odd endpoint participates.

lessons.title: Checkpoint: typed calculation boundary
lessons.body: Turn raw dimension text into a number-returning function and print its result. Parameter and return annotations expose a broken contract before execution; the branches and loops from the control-flow lab belong inside such boundaries.

lessons.title: Narrow a union before formatting
lessons.body: Represent parsed input as string | number, then use typeof so each branch calls methods available on its member. A runtime string must be validated before becoming a literal union; a cast alone cannot perform that check.

lessons.title: Validate text before a literal union
lessons.body: Accept only left or right after checking the actual stdin string, then call a Direction function. Writing `as Direction` changes the checker's view but neither tests nor changes the runtime value.

lessons.title: Default only nullish scores
lessons.body: Model an absent score as null and use ?? so the numeric value zero survives. Logical OR also replaces other falsy values, making it the wrong defaulting operator for a legitimate zero.
lessons.example:
const demoCount: number | undefined = 0;
process.stdout.write(`demo:${demoCount ?? 8}\n`);

lessons.title: Compose structural contracts
lessons.body: Combine an interface-provided name with a type-alias score through an intersection and format the resulting object. Interfaces may declaration-merge, aliases can name unions, and both remain checker-only structural descriptions; classes, readonly, and satisfies labs refine those boundaries.

lessons.title: Preserve an element type through first
lessons.body: Write first<T> so callers receive T | undefined without any, a cast, or a non-null assertion. keyof, indexed access, mapped, conditional, and utility types are optional transformations built on the same principle of deriving rather than repeating source types.

lessons.title: Guard a runtime key for keyof
lessons.body: Derive the key union from a limits value with keyof typeof, then narrow stdin with Object.hasOwn. Unlike the in operator, an own-property check rejects inherited names such as toString and constructor.

lessons.title: Reuse an array element type
lessons.body: Name a score with User["scores"][number] so the element type follows the model. That derived annotation still requires explicit conversion because stdin arrives as a string.

lessons.title: Map every feature key to a flag
lessons.body: Transform the Feature union into a complete boolean object, then report the enabled key. A mapped type requires both search and share, fixing the earlier impossible exercise whose requested edit and expected result disagreed.

lessons.title: Extract an element with a conditional type
lessons.body: Use infer inside a conditional type to derive the member of string[]. The conditional is evaluated only by the checker; it does not branch on stdin at runtime.

lessons.title: Patch selected fields with utility types
lessons.body: Represent an optional score update as Partial<Pick<User, "score">> and supply zero only when that property is absent. Partial changes static optionality; it neither inserts properties nor defaults runtime values.

lessons.title: Check routes while preserving literals
lessons.body: Define route strings with as const, check their Record shape through satisfies, and require Object.hasOwn before indexing external text. satisfies is not a cast, and as const neither freezes the object nor admits inherited keys.

lessons.title: Checkpoint: exhaust a tagged shape union
lessons.body: Parse rectangle and circle records into variants sharing a literal kind, then switch exhaustively with a never check. Literal tags enable sound narrowing, while satisfies can preserve such tags in configuration objects.

lessons.title: Await a typed promise
lessons.body: Return Promise<number> from an async function, await it in main, and attach a top-level rejection handler. Every async function returns a Promise even when its return statement names a plain number.

lessons.title: Orient a public API in a single-file runner
lessons.body: Model a public label function behind an explicit API object. This exercise intentionally does not claim multi-file import/export mastery: the judge runs one .ts file under an arbitrary nearest package.json, while real Node projects choose ESM or CommonJS through extensions and package metadata.

lessons.title: Capstone: validate and execute commands
lessons.body: Inputs use add or divide commands. Parse them into a discriminated union, reject partial or non-finite numbers, reject zero division, execute asynchronously, and narrow catch values from unknown. Number.parseInt accepts a numeric prefix such as 12px and returns NaN for bad text instead of throwing, so full-token validation must be explicit.
